trajectory_summary: The call opens on neutral ground and never builds energy. Bucky completes the verify section mechanically, burns seven-plus minutes on a bill-collection path that Shelly herself flags as unrepresentative, and attempts to book without ever articulating why the appointment would be worth Shelly's time. The withdrawal completes at 10:21 when Shelly declines the appointment, and escalates at 11:32 when she requests removal from all contact lists — a signal that the call's failure is compounded by a pre-existing contact-frequency irritant Bucky never surfaced or addressed.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends without a booked appointment — Shelly explicitly declines at 10:21 and requests contact removal at 11:32. The correct outcome tag is no_appointment / contact_removal_requested.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Value proposition for the appointment — curiosity creation before the ask
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Put me on the schedule. What does that mean?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Shelly's confusion at 10:07 is the clearest evidence that Bucky never answered the question every customer is silently asking: 'Why would I give you an hour of my time?' Seven minutes of bill navigation produced no curiosity, no value case, and no reason to say yes. This is the root cause of the no-appointment outcome — not the bill friction, not the family emergency, not the contact-frequency irritant. Those are compounding factors. The core failure is that Bucky treated the appointment as a procedural step rather than something worth wanting.

key_moments.quote: But it's on autopay, so I don't, like, keep it in my head.
key_moments.stewart_read: Autopay invisibility is the natural setup for the bill-pain inversion. Shelly is telling Bucky that her utility bill is a background cost she has stopped thinking about — which is precisely the emotional condition the flip is designed to activate. The move here is to surface the number she's been ignoring and make it feel real: 'That's actually the thing — most people on autopay are surprised when they see what they've been paying without noticing. The specialist's whole job is to show you that number and what solar does to it.' Bucky hears this as a data gap and moves on.

key_moments.quote: I wasn't at home for about six months because I was doing something else.
key_moments.stewart_read: Shelly discloses an extended absence from her home without explaining why. The disclosure is brief and she moves quickly to the practical implication ('this isn't going to be accurate'), which suggests she is not inviting a deep conversation — but she is offering context that deserves a human acknowledgment before the pivot. Bucky's response ('Oh, because you weren't in the home?') is a clarifying question, not an acknowledgment. The coaching point is the missed beat between the disclosure and the pivot, not speculation about what the absence involved.

key_moments.quote: That would be more helpful, but we can we can put you on the schedule if we get the last three months.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky attempts to book on imperfect data after seven minutes of bill-navigation friction. The strategic read is that Bucky recognized the data was imperfect and decided to book anyway, planning for the specialist to collect better data on-site. The reactive read is that Bucky had no value proposition to offer and defaulted to booking pressure as the only tool available. The reactive read is better supported: Bucky never explains what the appointment is for, never creates curiosity, and Shelly's immediate response ('Put me on the schedule. What does that mean?') confirms she has no idea why she would say yes. LEAD TYPE NOTE: the codex marks lead-type metadata as TBD for this call. If this is an organic lead, a bill is not required before booking — which would partially reframe this moment. The failure is the absent value proposition regardless of lead type; the bill-collection path itself may or may not have been mandatory.

key_moments.quote: Put me on the schedule. What does that mean?
key_moments.stewart_read: Shelly's question is not hostile — it is genuinely confused. She does not know what she is being asked to commit to. This is the direct result of Bucky never having explained the appointment's purpose or value at any point in the call. The codex's what_great_looks_like principle — legitimacy and curiosity established early — was never executed. By the time Bucky attempts to book, Shelly has no frame for what a 'specialist' is, what the call would cover, or why it would be worth her time.
